header.title: Resilient Mesh
header.subtitle: Chaos-resilient Java banking core with virtual-thread concurrency and production observability

summary.plain_english: A distributed banking backend simulation built to stay responsive during downstream failures and traffic spikes. It includes User, Wallet, and Transaction services with resilience controls, autoscaling-ready Kubernetes manifests, and a full metrics pipeline for incident diagnosis.

motivation.quote: I built Resilient Mesh to show I can design Java services for real production conditions, not just happy-path APIs: failures, retries, backpressure, and operational visibility from day one.

diagram.alt: Resilient Mesh architecture showing user, wallet, and transaction services, Prometheus/Grafana observability, and EKS deployment with HPA

architecture.flow_summary: Client request → transaction-service → wallet-service → user-service → resilience policies/fallbacks → metrics to Prometheus/Grafana

items: Serve high concurrent traffic with low thread overhead — Java 21 virtual threads (Project Loom) — avoids traditional thread-pool bottlenecks
Prevent cascading failures across dependencies — Resilience4j circuit breakers + retries with exponential backoff — keeps APIs responsive during partial outages
Expose runtime health and performance telemetry — Spring Actuator + Micrometer + Prometheus — enables fast triage of latency and JVM pressure
Run with production-safe Kubernetes defaults — requests/limits, readiness/liveness probes, JVM memory tuning — reduces OOM and startup instability
Scale elastically under load — HPA on CPU with path to custom Prometheus metrics — supports demand spikes without manual intervention
Keeping inter-service calls resilient without hiding real errors required careful retry limits and fallback behavior so failures degrade gracefully instead of masking incidents.
Tuning JVM memory for containers forced tradeoffs between throughput and stability; resource limits and `MaxRAMPercentage` had to align to avoid eviction or OOM kills.
Designing health probes for startup and runtime separately was necessary to prevent Kubernetes from recycling healthy but warming pods too aggressively.
Balancing useful portfolio scope against production realism meant prioritizing failure handling and observability before deeper business features.
What I'd do differently: add a persistence layer and idempotency strategy earlier so concurrency and consistency behavior can be benchmarked under write-heavy load.
